narration.default: You've heard it a hundred times — the beep that starts slow when you back into a parking spot, gets faster as the bumper gets closer to the wall, and turns into one solid scream right before you hit. Today you build that. Three behaviours from one sensor and one buzzer. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Distance Sensor extension is added in Sensor Manager. If you haven't done the earlier Arduino tutorials, finish Blink an LED and Button and LED first. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this a few times now — setup runs once at the start, loop runs forever while the Arduino has power. Everything we build today goes inside one of those two slots.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is going to be our trig pin — short for trigger. We'll see what it triggers in just a moment, but the program needs to know up front that pin 9 is going to send something out.
Drag a second pinMode block. Set pin to 10 and mode to input. Pin 10 is the echo pin — and yes, the names trig and echo are clues. One sends, one listens. Two pins, working as a pair.

steps.title: How does the Arduino see distance?

narration.default: Hold on a second. Your eyes work because light bounces off things and lands in them. But the Arduino has no eyes, no camera, no lens. So how on earth is it about to tell us how far away the wall is? The names trig and echo were the hint. Think for a moment before the next step gives it away.

It uses sound. The trig pin shouts a tiny chirp — too high-pitched for you to hear, but a real chirp. The chirp travels through the air, hits the wall, and bounces back. The echo pin listens for the bounce. The Arduino measures the time between shout and echo, multiplies by the speed of sound, and divides by two — because the chirp went there and back. That's the distance. Bats and dolphins navigate this exact way; we just borrowed their trick.

Drag a third pinMode block. Set pin to 3 and mode to output. Pin 3 is for the buzzer — that's the part that's actually going to make the beeping noise. Output, because we want to send a signal out to it. Three pinModes total — two for the sensor, one for the buzzer. Setup is done.

Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it distance. A variable is just a labelled box where the program can store a number and read it back later. Each loop, we're going to ask the sensor how far the wall is and stash that number in the distance box. Then the rest of the program reads from the box to decide what to do.

steps.title: Measure how far the wall is

narration.default: First, register the sensor in setup. Make a variable called sensorInit. Drag a 'set sensorInit to' block under the buzzer pinMode and from the Sensors category drop a distance sensor reporter into the value slot — set trig to 9 and echo to 10. The reporter creates a sensor and your variable now holds a reference to it. Now inside loop, drag a set distance to block, and from Sensors drop the measure distance reporter into its value slot, again with trig 9 and echo 10. Every loop the sensor fires a chirp, listens for the echo, and the answer in centimetres lands inside the distance box.

Pause. We want three different sounds based on how far the wall is. Far away — beep slowly, plenty of room. Getting closer — beep faster, you should slow down. Too close — solid tone, stop right now. Three ranges of distance, three rhythms. We'll build them with a chain of if-else blocks: ask one question, and if the answer is no, ask the next.

Drag an if-else block under the set-distance line. In the diamond, build the question distance greater than 100 — meaning the wall is more than 100 centimetres away. The next step fills in what to do when the answer is yes.

In the if branch, drop a tone block on pin 3 at 500 hertz, then a delay of 800 milliseconds, then a noTone block on pin 3, then another delay of 800 milliseconds. Beep — long pause — beep — long pause. That's the relaxed, you've-got-room rhythm.

Inside the else branch of the first if-else, drop another if-else. The new question: distance greater than 30. We already know it's not greater than 100 — that case was handled above — so this catches anywhere from 31 up to 100 centimetres.
In this if half, tone on pin 3 at 800 hertz, delay 200 milliseconds, noTone, delay 200 milliseconds. Same shape as before, just much shorter pauses — that's what makes it sound urgent.

In the inner else branch — the one that runs when distance is 30 or less — drop a single tone block on pin 3 at 1500 hertz. No delay, no noTone. The buzzer just holds one continuous high-pitched note. That's the stop right now scream. Three branches, three rhythms: slow beep when far away, fast beep when getting closer, solid tone when too close.

At the very bottom of loop — after the if-else chain ends — drag a delay block and set it to 50 milliseconds. Without this, loop would run thousands of times a second, asking the sensor for distance more often than it can answer. 50 milliseconds gives the sensor time to chirp and listen. Twenty distance checks a second is more than enough to feel instant.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Distance Sensor. The dialog asks for two pins this time, not one — set trig to 9 and echo to 10, and click Save. The virtual ultrasonic sensor appears, wired up to the same two pins your code is talking to.

Click the plus button again, choose Buzzer, set pin to 3, click Save. The virtual buzzer appears next to the sensor — same pin our tone and noTone blocks are sending to. Hardware ready. Code ready. Time to run it.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds to spin up. The simulator goes live.

The distance sensor in the simulator has a slider — that's how you pretend a wall is moving closer or further. Slide it all the way out: slow beep, long pauses. Slide it into the middle range: fast beep, short pauses. Slide it close: one solid scream. Three rhythms from one sensor, just by reading distance and choosing a branch.

Try this: add an LED on pin 5, give it a pinMode, and turn it on inside the close branch — now you have a buzzer and a warning light when the wall is too near. Or, instead of three fixed branches, use the map block to turn distance into a delay value smoothly — the closer the wall, the shorter the gap between beeps, with no sudden jumps. Same hardware, smarter behaviour.

Every modern car with reverse parking sensors does exactly this. So does the touchless soap dispenser, the automatic door at the supermarket, the robot vacuum that doesn't bump into chairs. Sensor checks distance, program decides what to do, output reacts — same shape, different jobs. Once you've built it once, you start spotting it everywhere.

Look at what you just learned. Ultrasonic sensing — chirp out, echo in, time-of-flight gives distance. Multi-branch decisions — if-elseif-else lets your program pick from three or more paths instead of just two. Tone variation — same buzzer, different frequencies and different delay rhythms make wildly different sounds. And sound-based sensing — a real, working alternative to eyes that you can program in twenty steps. Every parking sensor on every car on every road runs on this idea. You can build it now.
